% -----------------------------------------------------------------------------

As always, our approach will be hands-on and practical. We will guide you through a series of exercises designed to reinforce your understanding and proficiency in estimating the mean and proportion. Through real-world scenarios and practical applications, you will gain the skills and confidence needed to tackle estimation challenges in your own work.

\subsection*{Integration with R}

All calculations and analyses discussed in this chapter will be carried out using \ttblue{R}. We encourage you to follow along with the examples using your own \ttblue{R} environment, allowing you to actively engage with the material and gain firsthand experience in applying [topic] techniques.

Let's embark on this journey together as we explore the depths of eatimation with \ttblue{R} by our side.

\subsection{Case: Salaries}

\begin{exercise}

\textbf{Drawing a sample}

In this Chapter we will use one single data file, so you can get familiar with estimating a mean, a proportion, and their respective confidence intervals without learning the specifics of many different datasets. The \ttblue{salaries} dataset contains salary details of 2,222 employees of the imaginary town of Winesbury, and is included with the \ttblue{FSaudit} package. The data file indicates for each employee the Grade, Step within the Grade, and Gross salary, as well as a unique employee ID. The field Gender is coded with a 1 for female and a 0 for male.
\footnote{In order to replicate the experiment in the book, you need to use the same random number seed as we did. Note that as of version 3.6, fundamental changes have been made in the random number generator. The default setting of the random number generator should normally not be changed, but for the benefit of readers who use an older version of \ttblue{R} we set it to \ttblue{Rounding}, which was the default prior to version 3.6.}
Details of the data file can be obtained using base \ttblue{R} functionality.
\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{library(FSaudit)}
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{library(tibble)}
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{names(salaries)}
\textcolor{KPMG_light_blue}{[1] "grade" "step"  "gross" "id"    "sex"  }
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{(N <- nrow(salaries))}
\textcolor{KPMG_light_blue}{[1] 2222}
\end{Verbatim}

We sample 50 units from the \ttblue{salaries} dataset by using the \ttblue{sample()} function.

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{RNGkind(sample.kind = "Rounding")}
\textcolor{red}{Warning message:}
\textcolor{red}{In RNGkind(sample.kind = "Rounding") : non-uniform 'Rounding' }
\textcolor{red}{sampler used}
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{n <- 50}
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{set.seed(12345)}
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{salaries[2, 3]}
\textcolor{KPMG_light_blue}{# A tibble: 1 x 1}
\textcolor{KPMG_light_blue}{  gross}
\textcolor{KPMG_light_blue}{  <int>}
\textcolor{KPMG_light_blue}{1  5129}
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{sample1 <- salaries[sample(N, n), ]}
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{head(sample1)}
\textcolor{KPMG_light_blue}{# A tibble: 6 x 5}
\textcolor{KPMG_light_blue}{  grade  step gross     id   sex}
\textcolor{KPMG_light_blue}{  <chr> <int> <int>  <int> <int>}
\textcolor{KPMG_light_blue}{1 6         1  1612 910046     0}
\textcolor{KPMG_light_blue}{2 13        8  4818 536825     1}
\textcolor{KPMG_light_blue}{3 4         0  1399 468652     0}
\textcolor{KPMG_light_blue}{4 10        4  2845 890951     0}
\textcolor{KPMG_light_blue}{5 8         6  2486 688734     0}
\textcolor{KPMG_light_blue}{6 15        7  5585 798493     0}
\end{Verbatim}

\end{exercise}

\subsection{Section \ref{sec:point_estimation}}

\begin{exercise}

\textbf{Point estimate of the mean} 

The point estimate of the mean ($\bar{y}$) is obtained by dividing the sum of $y$ in the sample by the sample size. In \ttblue{R} we simply use the \ttblue{mean} function.

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{sum(sample1$gross)}
\textcolor{KPMG_light_blue}{[1] 150708}
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{(y\_bar <- mean(sample1$gross))}
\textcolor{KPMG_light_blue}{[1] 3014.16}
\end{Verbatim}

The estimate of the total monthly payroll is then obtained by multiplying the sample mean with the number of elements in the population $N$.

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{N * y_bar}
\textcolor{KPMG_light_blue}{[1] 6697464}
\end{Verbatim}

\end{exercise}

\subsection{Section \ref{sec:confidence_intervals}}

\begin{exercise}

\textbf{Confidence intervals around the mean}

First, we estimate the variance in the population from the variance of the sample.

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{(s2 <- var(sample1$gross))}
\textcolor{KPMG_light_blue}{[1] 2364064}
\end{Verbatim}

The calculation of the standard error is then

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{(se <- sqrt(s2 / n))}
\textcolor{KPMG_light_blue}{[1] 217.4426}
\end{Verbatim}

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{(tval <- qt(0.975, df = (n - 1)))}
\textcolor{KPMG_light_blue}{[1] 2.009575}
\end{Verbatim}

We multiply the $t$-value with the standard error to obtain the precision achieved.

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{(precAch <- tval * se)}
\textcolor{KPMG_light_blue}{[1] 436.9673}
\end{Verbatim}

The resulting lower and upper bounds are

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{(lowerBound <- y_bar - precAch)}
\textcolor{KPMG_light_blue}{[1] 2577.19}
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{(upperBound <- y_bar + precAch)}
\textcolor{KPMG_light_blue}{[1] 3453.11}
\end{Verbatim}

For a 99\% confidence interval, the calculations are

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{(lowerBound <- y_bar + qt(0.005, 49) * se)}
\textcolor{KPMG_light_blue}{[1] 2431.424}
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{(upperBound <- y_bar + qt(0.995, 49) * se)}
\textcolor{KPMG_light_blue}{[1] 3596.896}
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{N * lowerBound}
\textcolor{KPMG_light_blue}{[1] 5402625}
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{N * upperBound}
\textcolor{KPMG_light_blue}{[1] 7992302}
\end{Verbatim}

\end{exercise}

\subsection{Section \ref{sec:estimating_the_sample_size}}

\begin{exercise}

\textbf{Extending the sample}

We extend the sample from Exercise \ref{ex:sample} from 50 to 131 sampling units.

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{set.seed(12345)}
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{sample2 <- salaries[sample(N, 131), ]}
\end{Verbatim}

The mean of the total sample is:

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{mean(sample2$gross)}
\textcolor{KPMG_light_blue}{[1] 3332.137}
\end{Verbatim}

\end{exercise}

\begin{exercise}

\textbf{Finite populations}

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{E <- 300000}
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{tval <- qt(0.975, df = (n - 1))}
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{(gamma  <- E^2 * (N - 1) / (N^2 * tval^2 * var(sample1$gross)))}
\textcolor{KPMG_light_blue}{[1] 4.242586}
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{(N / (1 + gamma))}
\textcolor{KPMG_light_blue}{[1] 423.8366}
\end{Verbatim}

The total sample size is then 524:

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{(N^2 * tval^2) * var(sample1$gross)/E^2}
\textcolor{KPMG_light_blue}{[1] 523.7372}
\end{Verbatim}

If we do not take the finite-population corrector factor, the total sample size is larger.

\end{exercise}

\subsection{Section \ref{sec:confidence_intervals_around_the_proportion}}

\begin{exercise}

\textbf{Confidence intervals around the proportion}

These bounds are calculated in \ttblue{R} with the \ttblue{upper} and \ttblue{lower} functions, that are part of the \ttblue{FSaudit} package. These two function use the hypergeometric distribution by default.

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{lower(k = 31, popn = 2222, n = 50, alpha = .025)}
\textcolor{KPMG_light_blue}{[1] 1052}
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{upper(k = 31, popn = 2222, n = 50, alpha = .025)}
\textcolor{KPMG_light_blue}{[1] 1671}
\end{Verbatim}
\index[FSaudit]{\ttblue{lower}}\index[FSaudit]{\ttblue{upper}}

Binomial bounds can be obtained in \ttblue{R}, by adding the distribution to the respective functions.

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{lower(k = 31, n = 50, alpha = .025, dist = "binom")}
\textcolor{KPMG_light_blue}{[1] 0.4717492}
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{upper(k = 31, n = 50, alpha = .025, dist = "binom")}
\textcolor{KPMG_light_blue}{[1] 0.7534989}
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{lower(k = 31, popn = 2222, n = 50, alpha = .025, dist = "binom")}
\textcolor{KPMG_light_blue}{[1] 1048}
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{upper(k = 31, popn = 2222, n = 50, alpha = .025, dist = "binom")}
\textcolor{KPMG_light_blue}{[1] 1675}
\end{Verbatim}
\index[FSaudit]{\ttblue{lower}}\index[FSaudit]{\ttblue{upper}}

\end{exercise}

Note that the hypergeometric distribution provides a more precise result.
